LinkedIn respects your privacy

LinkedIn and 3rd parties use essential and non-essential cookies to provide, secure, analyze and improve our Services, and to show you relevant ads (including professional and job ads) on and off LinkedIn. Learn more in our Cookie Policy.

Select Accept to consent or Reject to decline non-essential cookies for this use. You can update your choices at any time in your settings.

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

Skip to main content
LinkedIn
  • Top Content
  • People
  • Learning
  • Jobs
  • Games
Join now Sign in

Jordan Hallow’s Post

Jordan Hallow
1y Edited
  • Report this post

Is "Easy Apply" worth it for job seekers? As a recruiter who has used it for recruiting, here are my thoughts: Let's talk about what recruiters see when you use Easy Apply: -Profile picture -Headline -4 most recent work experiences (job titles + company) -Responses to screening questions (if they have them) -Education -Skills/Relevant keywords -Contact info That's it See one thing that's missing from that list? Your resume So most are getting rejected before their resume is reviewed What else? LinkedIn will put applicants into a category based on fit Just by looking at a snapshot of your profile Especially with Easy Apply where hundreds (if not thousands) are applying For us to review your resume We have to agree to move forward based on what we see And you have to be categorized as being a good fit for the role Based on your LinkedIn profile snapshot So...is it worth it? Well....no not for most people BUT I would recommend it as part of your strategy if you do the following: -Update your headshot -Build a headline that showcases your value + roles you are interested in (For the love of Pete, stop putting "Open to new opportunities" in your headline. It's taking up valuable space) -Identify the keywords/skills from the job description (hint: hard skills) and ensure you have them visible in multiple places on your profile -Make sure job titles are relevant -Using it to identify who the hiring manager/recruiter is and sending them a message Using Easy Apply means that everyone else is using it Easy Apply = Easy Reject If you're doing what everyone else is doing It's hard to stand out from the crowd Which defeats the entire purpose of the job search process Do you use LinkedIn's Easy Apply feature to apply for jobs? Have you experienced any success with it? Let me know in the comments

296 100 Comments
Like Comment
Amanda Tobe, C.Psych, PhD 1y
  • Report this comment

Easy Apply can be tempting, but it often puts you in a sea of applicants. For real impact, I always suggest my clients to take a creative approach: connect with hiring managers, optimize their profiles with tailored content, and use their network to stand out. Going beyond the easy route is what truly gets noticed.

Like
Reply
5 Reactions 6 Reactions
Joseph Howard 1y
  • Report this comment

This is helpful to know Jordan Hallow. Would a better alternative be to go directly to the company web site and apply from there? Anything else you recommend?

Like
Reply
4 Reactions 5 Reactions
Matt Tooker 1y
  • Report this comment

This insider view, which I happen to share is insightful and appreciated Jordan. It does speak to the need to have a fully optimized profile but not so you can pass the Easy Apply screen. So that when a recruiter is doing a search they can easily find you. Easy apply creates the false narrative that you are “busy” in your job search, “busy” applying to roles. Instead how about applying to fewer roles and making your candidacy stand out. Great topic!

Like
Reply
5 Reactions 6 Reactions
Keturah Roberts 1y
  • Report this comment

We hire folks who use Easy Apply and I've gotten interviews in the past using Easy Apply. The recruiter can set up application questions to further narrow down the applicant pool because face it, many completely unqualified people will Easy Apply to roles that make no sense for them. It might not be optimal for either party, but definitely still worth applying if you meet many of the required qualifications!

Like
Reply
16 Reactions 17 Reactions
Sean Dower 1y
  • Report this comment

So when it asks for my resume its not sending it?

Like
Reply
10 Reactions 11 Reactions
David Hannan 1y
  • Report this comment

Easy apply is a one-way ticket to blend in with everyone else’s strategy, Jordan. If it’s the only hand you’re playing, don’t expect to be at the table for long.

Like
Reply
3 Reactions 4 Reactions
Jon T. 1y
  • Report this comment

I don't believe a recruiter has ever reached out to me when I've used the Easy Apply button.

Like
Reply
2 Reactions 3 Reactions
Dominic Imwalle 1y
  • Report this comment

Why not have those things up to date? You gain everything by staying ready. I'm definitely not a fan of this approach, but if the opportunity comes up and you move fast on a role - you want that first impression to go well. Nice post Jordan!

Like
Reply
2 Reactions 3 Reactions
David Fischer 1y
  • Report this comment

Jordan Hallow: I often hear different opinions about the 'Easy Apply' feature. One recruiter shares their perspective, while another has a different take. I've also been encouraged to apply directly through the company's website. What are your thoughts on this?

Wow, I didn’t know this. Thanks for sharing. That means marketing oneself / storytelling work experiences through linkedin is SUPER important.

More Relevant Posts

  • Aakriti Khosla
    8mo Edited
    • Report this post

    𝟮𝟬 𝘄𝗮𝘆𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝗴𝗲𝘁 𝘀𝗲𝗲𝗻 𝗶𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗷𝗼𝗯 𝗺𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘁 You have zero online presence for recruiters to find. → Comment on three posts in your industry every day. You're not following up after you apply to jobs. → Send a follow up message three days after applying. You're listing tasks instead of showing real results achieved. → Use numbers to prove what you actually accomplished there. You think networking means asking people for jobs directly. → Ask for advice and informational chats first - build credibility & trust Job leads will follow once they know you enough You're apologizing for gaps in your work history timeline. → Explain what you learned during that time instead confidently. Your resume sounds like a robot. → Write it like you talk to real people daily. You're applying through job portals only. → Message hiring managers directly on LinkedIn instead every time. Your LinkedIn headline is just your job title. → Say what problem you solve for companies right now. You're waiting for perfect job postings to appear. → Apply even when you meet 70% of the requirements. Your cover letter is three long paragraphs nobody reads. → Write five short lines that show your exact value. You have no proof of your wins anywhere online. → Post one project or result you're proud of today. You're using the same resume for every single job. → Change three lines to match each job you want. You're scared to reach out to strangers for help. → Send ten cold messages this week asking for advice. Your LinkedIn profile has no personality at all showing. → Add your story and why you do this work. You're only applying to jobs posted this week online. → Find companies you love and message them directly without posts. You haven't told anyone you're looking for work yet. → Post that you're open to opportunities right now today. You're hiding skills because you're not an expert yet. → List every skill you've used even once before successfully. Your network thinks you're happy where you are now. → Tell five people today exactly what you're looking for. You're ignoring smaller companies in your job search completely. → Small companies hire faster and see you more clearly. You're giving up after twenty rejections hit your inbox. → The right yes only needs to happen one time. The system filters you out. But people let you in. Stop playing by rules that weren't made for you anyway. 𝗚𝗲𝘁 𝗹𝗼𝘂𝗱. 𝗚𝗲𝘁 𝘀𝗽𝗲𝗰𝗶𝗳𝗶𝗰. 𝗚𝗲𝘁 𝘀𝗲𝗲𝗻. Your next role is waiting for someone exactly like you. --------------------------------------------- 🌟 Hi, I’m Aakriti 🎯 Topmate’s Top 1% | Certified Career & Life Coach 🛫 Helping you launch corporate careers 📈 Follow along for Insider recruiting lens & strategies to land the job offer

    5
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Amit Patel
    8mo
    • Report this post

    A candidate told me last week she spent 80 hours in September applying to online job posts. 127 applications. Three callbacks total. No offers. I asked how much time she spent networking, reaching former colleagues, and contacting hiring managers directly. "Maybe an hour." I followed up to ask why she would leave her valuable network, built over 12 years of work, heavy travel, and an MBA program, untouched. Silence. It's not just the job market. You need to run plays that score. Online postings fill 30% of jobs. Low quality-to-quantity ratio. The other 70% happens elsewhere. 30% from internal referrals, networking, and agency recruiters. Highest conversion rate per applicant. 40% come from internal channels (company career sites, internal moves, campus recruiting, direct sourcing, etc.). Brand power and infrastructure wins here. Some candidates spend the majority of their time on online postings, while they have the most control and conversion in the 30% referral channel. Not the algorithm, ATS filter, or screening tool. Their network. July JOLTS showed a 4.1% job opening rate. Quit rate dropped to 2.2%. People are job-hugging. Over the past year, ~75% of U.S. employers have been or are currently in hiring mode. Among those, ~80% report difficulty finding qualified candidates. (All data derived from estimates across credible sources; no unified dataset exists) Here's what's causing that disconnect: -50% is a genuine skills or experience mismatch. -35% is matching inefficiency and job search frictions. -15% is suboptimal search behavior and screening filters. Translation: the process sucks. Filtering through ghost jobs, resume buzzword stuffing, and job application black holes. If pursuing a job with 800 applicants isn't working, try a new approach: -40% on networking and relationship-building. Reach out to past colleagues. Ask for intros. Connect with alumni in your target companies. Schedule informational interviews. Cultivate new connections deliberately. Many jobs aren't advertised publicly. This is your highest-leverage channel. -30% on targeted, high-quality applications. Focus on fit. Research and tailor your resume. Reach out to the recruiter or hiring manager if you can. Some will be perturbed by your audacity. Be tenacious or be a long-term job seeker. Quality over quantity. -30% on proactive outreach and supplementary channels. Position for roles that aren't yet advertised. Cold outreach to hiring managers at target companies. Express interest. Knock on doors. Work selectively with recruiters in your field. Attend events. Place yourself where the puck is going. There is no one-size-fits-all. A/B test. Fail fast. Track what works. Shift time to what produces (80/20). Easy to find the front door with the long line. Difficult to enter. The side door requires more effort to find. Much easier to walk in.

    165 27 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Scott Borden LPC, NCC
    9mo
    • Report this post

    “There are a lot of tools, apps, and websites that you can use to help with a job search. Here are some that can help you streamline your job search and get hired faster.” Thanks for sharing Alison Doyle

    Alison Doyle

    Co-Founder, The Job Hopper

    9mo Edited

    There are a lot of tools, apps, and websites that you can use to help with a job search. Here are some that can help you streamline your job search and get hired faster. ✅ When you're looking for a job in a specific industry or career field, using job sites that focus on the types of jobs you're interested in can be a time-saver. Plus, you'll find postings that aren't listed on sites like Indeed and LinkedIn. Job Search Database features links to 1,000+ niche career sites, organized by category. It's one of the best resources for finding job listings, and it's free. https://lnkd.in/eYtwd-dP ✅ Huntr helps you create tailored resumes and cover letters fast with AI, fill out application forms in one click, and automatically organize your job search. Sign up for free. https://huntr.co/?via=jen ✅ Also from Huntr, here's a three-step data-backed job search process that works, and you can message Huntr's Sam Wright for a free strategy session if you need more help. https://lnkd.in/eusHMVvD ✅ Google's free tools will help grow your career and find your next job. Explore career options, take a course, earn a certificate, write your resume and cover letters, track your applications, find the latest job listings, and practice interviewing with Google's tools. https://lnkd.in/eDAPcYNh ✅ Stressed about interviewing? There are free online tools that help you practice answering questions and get ready to ace the interview. Here's a selection of free interview practice tools to try. https://lnkd.in/enesQimE ✅ Proficiently.com combines personal career coaching with AI to help you get hired. Proficiently provides personalized job curation, job matching, resume and cover letter assistance, networking outreach, LinkedIn optimization, interview prep, and more. https://lnkd.in/gHMWdZSq ✅ Here are even more tools to streamline your job search, including a checklist of what you need to get started, resume and cover letter writing and editing tools, the best ways to find relevant job postings, and apps to manage and track your job search. https://lnkd.in/gD7-KKYf

    There are a lot of tools, apps, and websites that you can use to help with a job search. Here are some that can help you streamline your job search and get hired faster. ✅ When you're looking for a job in a specific industry or career field, using job sites that focus on the types of jobs you're interested in can be a time-saver. Plus, you'll find postings that aren't listed on sites like Indeed and LinkedIn. Job Search Database features links to 1,000+ niche career sites, organized by category. It's one of the best resources for finding job listings, and it's free. https://lnkd.in/eYtwd-dP ✅ Huntr helps you create tailored resumes and cover letters fast with AI, fill out application forms in one click, and automatically organize your job search. Sign up for free. https://huntr.co/?via=jen ✅ Also from Huntr, here's a three-step data-backed job search process that works, and you can message Huntr's Sam Wright for a free strategy session if you need more help. https://lnkd.in/eusHMVvD ✅ Google's free tools will help grow your career and find your next job.     54 5 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Wepply

    1,470 followers

    8mo
    • Report this post

    Here are the things every jobseekers should do before applying for a role At Wepply, we’ve seen it countless times: job seekers applying for different roles with the same CV. It’s easy to assume one CV fits all. But here’s the truth: every role is different. Every company has its own priorities. And every recruiter is looking for something specific. So before you hit that “Apply” button, take a few minutes to go through these steps 👉Read the job description slowly. Most job seekers rush through this part, but that’s where the secret lies. Every word in that job post tells you something about what the company values. Look out for repeated words or phrases; those are clues. If a company keeps mentioning “team collaboration” or “project management,” they want someone who truly embodies those skills. When you understand what the employer needs, you’ll know how to shape your CV to meet that need. 👉Adjust your CV for each application. You don’t need to rewrite your entire CV, just refine it. Rearrange your experiences so that the most relevant ones come first. If you once worked on a project that aligns with what the new role demands, bring it forward. Tailoring your CV shows effort. It tells the recruiter, “I didn’t just click apply, I read, understood, and showed you why I fit.” 👉Update your professional summary. Your summary is the first thing a recruiter reads; it sets the tone. Instead of keeping it general, let it reflect your readiness for that specific role. For example: Instead of saying, “Experienced administrative officer with 4 years of experience,” You could say, “Administrative professional with 4 years of experience managing client records and supporting cross-functional teams now seeking to bring that efficiency to XYZ Company.” It’s subtle, but it makes all the difference. 👉Add results, not just responsibilities. Recruiters love to see impact, not just activity. Don’t only say what you did, show what changed because you did it. For instance, ❌ “Handled customer inquiries.” ✅ “Resolved over 100 customer inquiries weekly, improving satisfaction ratings by 25%.” Numbers make your work tangible. They tell your story with evidence. 👉Keep the format simple and readable. ⁠You don’t need fancy designs or colorful templates. Sometimes, the most professional CVs are the cleanest ones. Avoid tables, images, and text boxes; many applicant tracking systems (ATS) can’t read them. 👉Stick to a simple structure: Summary → Experience → Education → Skills. Your CV should be easy on the eyes, both for recruiters and technology. At Wepply, we believe your CV is more than just a document. It’s your story told clearly, confidently, and intentionally. So before you send your next application, take a few minutes to review your CV using these steps. It might just be the difference between being unseen and being invited. #Wepply #JobSearch #CareerTips #CVWriting #ResumeHelp #JobSeekers

    5 2 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Sathish Reddy
    8mo Edited
    • Report this post

    Title: The Hidden Job Story – How Rohan Found His Dream Job Beyond the Online Crowd Rohan was like thousands of job seekers. Every morning, he opened job portals — Naukri, LinkedIn, Indeed — and applied for every job that matched his title: “HR Executive.” But no calls came. He noticed something — for one HR role, over 1200 people had already applied. That’s when he realized — applying online isn’t enough. 🌪️ The Competition Trap When you apply through portals, your resume goes into a system (ATS). If it doesn’t match 80–90% of the keywords, it never reaches the recruiter’s eyes. Even if it does, recruiters often pick the first 30–40 applications. So Rohan decided to change his strategy. 🌟 Step 1: Find the Hidden Jobs Most jobs are never posted online. They’re filled through: Internal references LinkedIn networking Recruiter WhatsApp groups Company career pages HR communities on Telegram/LinkedIn 👉 Rohan joined HR & Job WhatsApp groups, followed company recruiters, and commented on their posts. He even sent connection requests with a short message like: “Hi, I’m Rohan — HR Executive with 4 years of experience in recruitment and onboarding. Would love to stay connected for any openings at your organization.” That single line opened doors to hidden opportunities. 💡 Step 2: Apply Smart, Not Fast Instead of clicking “Apply All,” Rohan started customizing each application: Tailored resume for every job. Used keywords from the JD. Sent a follow-up message on LinkedIn to the recruiter after applying. This helped his profile stand out among 1000+ applicants. 🚪 Step 3: Build Visibility He started posting short updates on LinkedIn — tips about interviews, his learning journey, and gratitude posts about past work. Within a month, recruiters began approaching him directly. 🎯 Step 4: Use Job Channels Beyond Portals Rohan discovered: LinkedIn “Open to Work” feature works only if your profile is optimized. Job communities like Minaukri, MyNoticePeriod, Relevel, and AngelList have faster recruiter reach. Direct emails to HRs listed on company websites often go unnoticed — unless your subject line is strong. Example subject line: “Immediate Joiner | HR Executive | 4 Yrs Experience | Interested in [Company Name]” 🌈 The Result After three weeks, Rohan got two interview calls — not from job portals, but through a LinkedIn HR group where he had commented helpfully. He realized something powerful: “Jobs aren’t always online — they’re hidden behind networks.” 💬 Moral of the Story To win in a world of 1000 applicants: Network > Apply. Personalize > Copy-Paste. Visibility > Waiting. Hidden jobs are not hidden — they’re just shared where you haven’t looked yet. #hiring #opentowork #jobs #interviews #workfromhome #wfh #career #fresher

    1 Comment
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Sanyam Sareen
    9mo
    • Report this post

    Do you know what happens to your job application after you hit ‘Apply’? (The truth will shock you.) Most people don’t know where their application goes when they submit it. And in this market, that gap in understanding is costing people time, energy, and confidence. Here’s what actually happens, especially in 2025, when recruiters are heavily dependent on AI. You apply. Your resume goes into an Applicant Tracking System (ATS). It’s scanned for keywords, job titles, skills, and relevance. Sometimes, it gets ranked. Sometimes, it gets filtered out before a recruiter ever sees it. If your resume doesn’t match the language in the job description, or if it looks too generic, it might not even make it to a human. And when it does, you’re one of 500+ other applicants in line. Recruiters are short on time. Hiring teams are stretched thin. And yes, some sourcing is now powered by AI. So if you’re wondering why you’re not hearing back, it’s not always about you. Sometimes, your application didn’t even get seen. That’s why in today’s job market, applying alone isn’t enough. → You need a resume that’s aligned with the role, not just your history. → You need visibility through comments, thoughtful engagement, referrals, and follow-ups. → You need to treat job searching like a system, not a shot in the dark. You don’t need to apply to 100 roles. You need to be seen by the right 10, and heard by at least 2. If you’re sending out applications and getting silence, there’s a better way. I’ve built a system that has helped 500+ job seekers stand out and get heard. P.S. Want the exact system? Follow me and comment “SYSTEM”, and I’ll share it with you. 

    40 13 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Steve Quinn, MBA
    8mo
    • Report this post

    Job searching can feel like juggling knives: writing a resume that actually tells your story, decoding ATS and keywords, and hopping between job boards all while keeping your confidence intact. I’ve seen bright, qualified candidates get worn down more by the process than by rejections. To include myself. Here’s a practical, calmer way through it. Start with clarity Define 2–3 roles/industries you actually want. Focus beats scattering applications. Create a single “master résumé” with every accomplishment, then tailor from that. Make your résumé work for you. Lead with impact: results, metrics, and clear outcomes (not just duties). Optimize for ATS: use keywords from the job posting naturally; avoid fancy formatting that breaks parsing. Keep a one-page version for recruiters and a longer version for interviews/portfolios. Work the boards — smarter, not harder. Pick 2–3 job sites that serve your field and set targeted alerts. Use company career pages and LinkedIn Saved Searches for better leads. Track applications in a simple sheet: role, date, status, follow-up planned. Protect your time and sanity. Time-block application work (e.g., 90 minutes/day) and limit daily submissions. Celebrate small wins: replies, interviews, network connections. Build a support system: mentors, peers, or a coach for feedback and accountability. Turn rejection into data. Note patterns (common feedback, missing skills). Fill gaps with microprojects, online courses, or freelance work you can list on your résumé. If you’re in the middle of this grind: pick one thing from the list above and try it this week. Share your experience and what helped you most during a job search. #JobSearch #CareerAdvice #Resumes

    2
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Zarélsie Van der Merwe
    8mo
    • Report this post

    ____"I have applied for every single available job on SEEK". YIKES! I spoke to a candidate today on a Career Visibility Strategy Call. She sent over 50 job applications to SEEK job ads a week. She has been at it for 3 months! The result … nothing, zilch, nada, zero replies. Cold rejections, mostly nothing. I asked if she had ever considered another job search strategy. "What other strategy?" – she looked perplexed. As I explained that in her industry, fewer than 10% of jobs are ever advertised, I could see her face drop… …and then I explained how employers in that industry typically hire, …her eyes lit up. "So, are there more I can apply for?" Sigh! No – stop thinking "apply for jobs online" and let's go outside this box.... it just means that there is a hidden pool of opportunities available – but you cannot get to them by searching for online job ads… So, we did a few quick exercises and uncovered some of them… 1. In Google (Boolean search): " "[your occupation]" and "talent pool" " + set location in Advanced filters, e.g. …we found 10+ pages of listings, went through the first 15, and only ONE was advertised on SEEK… Why? Companies steer away from paying for expensive job ads on SEEK and will first display opportunities on their website. Sometimes these can be an ongoing invitation for new candidates, so they can fill their talent pools. 2. In LinkedIn (Boolean search) : " "occupation" and "location" ", then >Posts. It turns out there were 9+ pages of where individuals (hiring managers) posted opportunities in their feeds (not LinkedIn jobs), because they steer away from paying for expensive LinkedIn job ads. We found 7 instances of these…posted in the last 2 months. 3. In Google (Boolean search): " ""expression(s) of interest" and "[occupation]" + location settings. It revealed several organisations with Expressions of Interest called for on their website, with several opportunities! 4. LinkedIn Analytics: She already had a value profile built, and never checked who was viewing her profile. We went digging in there… Turns out at least 12 hiring managers from ideal companies viewed her profile. I then showed her how to start engaging and interacting with them - meaningfully. They are all warm leads! The result – she started conversations with some of them. …she had numerous opportunities to follow up on! NO job ads on SEEK … NO "tailored" CVs … NO competitors … In most instances, small companies that asked for a CV to be emailed – NO ATS either! Two weeks later, she had two interviews lined up. Thinking outside the advertised-job-ads-on-job-boards-box uncovered a part of the hidden job market, and …she has only just scratched the surface. P.S. test this and let me know how it works for you ;)

    19 5 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • The Elite Career Mentor

    98 followers

    8mo
    • Report this post

    ____"I have applied for every single available job on SEEK". YIKES! I spoke to a candidate today on a Career Visibility Strategy Call. She sent over 50 job applications to SEEK job ads a week. She has been at it for 3 months! The result … nothing, zilch, nada, zero replies. Cold rejections, mostly nothing. I asked if she had ever considered another job search strategy. "What other strategy?" – she looked perplexed. As I explained that in her industry, fewer than 10% of jobs are ever advertised, I could see her face drop… …and then I explained how employers in that industry typically hire, …her eyes lit up. "So, are there more I can apply for?" Sigh! No – stop thinking "apply for jobs online" and let's go outside this box.... it just means that there is a hidden pool of opportunities available – but you cannot get to them by searching for online job ads… So, we did a few quick exercises and uncovered some of them… 1. In Google (Boolean search): " "[your occupation]" and "talent pool" " + set location in Advanced filters, e.g. …we found 10+ pages of listings, went through the first 15, and only ONE was advertised on SEEK… Why? Companies steer away from paying for expensive job ads on SEEK and will first display opportunities on their website. Sometimes these can be an ongoing invitation for new candidates, so they can fill their talent pools. 2. In LinkedIn (Boolean search) : " "occupation" and "location" ", then >Posts. It turns out there were 9+ pages of where individuals (hiring managers) posted opportunities in their feeds (not LinkedIn jobs), because they steer away from paying for expensive LinkedIn job ads. We found 7 instances of these…posted in the last 2 months. 3. In Google (Boolean search): " ""expression(s) of interest" and "[occupation]" + location settings. It revealed several organisations with Expressions of Interest called for on their website, with several opportunities! 4. LinkedIn Analytics: She already had a value profile built, and never checked who was viewing her profile. We went digging in there… Turns out at least 12 hiring managers from ideal companies viewed her profile. I then showed her how to start engaging and interacting with them - meaningfully. They are all warm leads! The result – she started conversations with some of them. …she had numerous opportunities to follow up on! NO job ads on SEEK … NO "tailored" CVs … NO competitors … In most instances, small companies that asked for a CV to be emailed – NO ATS either! Two weeks later, she had two interviews lined up. Thinking outside the advertised-job-ads-on-job-boards-box uncovered a part of the hidden job market, and …she has only just scratched the surface. P.S. test this and let me know how it works for you ;)
